topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, the familiar dread settling in my chest like a cold stone. I clicked it open, my fingers hovering over the screen, tracing the unfamiliar name that adorned the byline. It was mine. My words, my careful observations, now belonging to someone else. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound that seemed too large for the small apartment. I reread the text, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. Every sentence felt tainted, a betrayal of the hours I’d poured into crafting them. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath my weight, and stared blankly at the dusty windowpane, watching the city lights blur into streaks of indifferent color. There was nothing left to fight with.

[story 2]

I picked up the printed copy, the paper feeling thin and fragile beneath my fingertips. The signature on the masthead was wrong. A name I didn't recognize, a ghost claiming my voice. A tightness gripped my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I crumpled the paper once, then smoothed it out again, the crease sharp against my palm. I walked to the kitchen, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic rhythm of my pulse. I poured myself a glass of water, the ice clinking with a brittle sound. I didn't feel angry, just profoundly empty. It was as if a vital part of me had been subtly hollowed out, leaving only a residue of exhaustion behind.

[story 3]

The notification flashed, a stark white intrusion on the muted backdrop of my evening. My gaze drifted from the screen to the stack of unpaid bills on the desk, each one a small, insistent weight. When I finally focused back on the article, the realization hit not as a shock, but as a slow, sinking realization. Someone else had taken the shape of my thoughts and published them. A deep, internal slump occurred, a physical yielding to the sheer weight of the situation. I didn't move to delete the email or contact anyone. Instead, I simply sat down heavily on the edge of the sofa, letting the tension drain out of my shoulders, allowing the quiet acceptance of defeat to wash over me like a heavy, slow tide.
